danger_signals.visual_form: Flames or smoke shooting from rear of car
danger_signals.meaning: Vehicle mechanical failure; imminent retirement from race
Gearbox or electronic system failure; vehicle damage state

danger_signals.visual_form: Vehicle parked on side of road or in pits after failure
danger_signals.meaning: Permanent race retirement; mechanical DNF state

danger_signals.visual_form: Vehicle deformation and sudden position change
danger_signals.meaning: Collision damage; temporary or permanent impact on race continuation

safety_signals.visual_form: Vehicle maintaining normal trajectory on racing line
safety_signals.meaning: Safe driving state; no mechanical or collision hazards

safety_signals.visual_form: Vehicle in designated pit area during repairs/refueling
safety_signals.meaning: Safe zone for mechanical work and strategy execution

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Team livery (non-tobacco variant, 1994 German GP spec)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Damon Hill (Car 0) and David Coulthard (Car 2) driver numbers
Andrea de Cesaris (Car 29) and Heinz-Harald Frentzen (Car 30)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Standardized non-tobacco liveries across all 14 teams
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: 1994 German Grand Prix livery specification applied uniformly

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Track environment rendered with standard conditions; single vehicle focus on track
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: AI drivers occupy circuit; player can cycle through practice, then qualifying session sequence

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Full 16-circuit championship grid displayed; multiple teams/drivers visible on track simultaneously
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Simulated race progression with lap counting, pit strategy opportunities, mechanical failures triggered stochastically

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Clean car visuals; no smoke/flame effects; standard paint scheme fully visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Normal acceleration, braking, and handling response; no performance degradation

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Flames or heavy smoke ejecting from rear engine bay; visual damage indicator
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Vehicle continues limited track operation but with compromised performance; eventual pit parking or track retirement

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Off-track detection triggered (visual track boundary);  no black flag visual shown
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Vehicle speed reduced for limited duration (penalty enforcement); automatic reset without race disqualification

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Track changes across 16 circuits (e.g., Silverstone, Estoril, Jerez show mid-season safety modifications; Barcelona, Montreal, Spa show original spec)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Points accumulation across full season simulation; driver/team standings dynamically updated

environmental_storytelling.example: Silverstone, Estoril, and Jerez circuits display safety-related track modifications made during 1994 season in-game; Barcelona, Montreal, and Spa retain original layouts—visually communicating real FIA regulatory timeline
All team cars run non-tobacco liveries reflecting 1994 German Grand Prix specification, visually encoding sponsor/regulatory context of that specific race moment in F1 history
Cars exhibit visual degradation (smoke, flames) before parking permanently on track side or in pits, communicating mechanical attrition as core race narrative element

environmental_storytelling.visual_manifestation: Simulated TV coverage angles alongside cockpit POV; environmental details visible in broadcast-style camera, reinforcing authenticity of F1 presentation

environmental_storytelling.example: Driver number assignment (0-34) and car position on grid visually identifies team hierarchy and competitive standing before race start

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Responds to steering, throttle, and brake inputs; visual trajectory updates in real-time based on setup configuration
Simulated driving behavior adapts to circuit and difficulty level; visible on-track positioning relative to player
Smoke/flame visual effects persist; vehicle continues moving but exits racing line; progresses toward pit parking or track-side retirement
Temporary or permanent loss of control; visual deformation possible; returns to garage or stays disabled based on severity
Speed reduction applied automatically; no visual penalty flag shown; recovery acceleration resumes after time penalty expires

notes: Grand Prix 2 communicates world-state primarily through mechanical failure visualization (smoke/flames as danger signals) and consistent team livery design reflecting real 1994 F1 regulatory context. The game lacks explicit visual penalty systems (no black flag graphic for rule violations) but compensates with performance-based enforcement (speed reduction). Circuit environmental storytelling is subtle but historically accurate—safety modifications visible in specific track versions encode the 1994 season's real-world evolution. The faction/team identification system relies entirely on livery standardization (1994 German GP spec across all 16 circuits/14 teams) rather than dynamic visual variation. No weather state-change visuals documented in source material.
